coredata.dc:title: A colorimetric sensor based on multiple elements doped carbon dot nanozyme for rapid detection of 1-naphthol in human urine samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Nie, Linchun
Li, Shuangying
Jiang, Liushan
Bu, Lutong
Dong, Guangyu
Song, Denghao
Liao, Jiawei
Tang, Guojin
Zhou, Qingxiang
coredata.dc:description:
The residual carbaryl in crops can cause serious damage to the human kidney and nervous system after entering the human body, which may be metabolized to 1-naphthol (1-NAP) and excreted through urine. 1-NAP is often used as the biomarker for carbaryl exposure, so the intake or leakage of carbaryl can be monitored by detecting the concentration of 1-NAP. Herein, Co, N, P ternary co-doped carbon dots (CoNP-CDs) derived from vitamin B12 were synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method. CoNP-CDs exhibited oxidase-like activity and excellent peroxidase-like activity, which was attributed to the Fenton-like reaction of Co2+/Co3+ and the presence of pyrrole N and P elements, which together provided multiple active sites for chromogenic substrates. Due to the dual enzyme-like activity of CoNP-CDs, hydroxyl radicals (
OH) and superoxide radicals (
O2 −) were generated during the catalytic process, which could rapidly oxidize colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethyl benzidine (TMB) to blue oxidation products (oxTMB). The α-carbon in 1-NAP can be attacked by
OH, and the catalytic oxidation process of TMB can be inhibited by the consumption of
OH, so that the blue color of the solution became lighter. Based on this principle, a smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensing platform was constructed for the detection of 1-NAP, and which resulted in a linear range of 1.07–37.3 μM and a visual detection limit of 0.68 μM. Moreover, the colorimetric sensing system showed satisfactory recoveries in the detection of human urine samples. The colorimetric sensing system owned the advantages of fast response, strong selectivity and simple operation, and provided a potential strategy for the on-site detection of 1-NAP.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multiple active sites
Peroxidase-like activity
1-NAP
Rapid response
Human urine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Colorimetric detection of 1-NAP by spectroscopy and smartphone
Oxidase-like activity and peroxidase-like activity of CoNP-CDs
Colorimetric detection of 1-NAP by UV–visible spectroscopy and smartphone
Detection of 1-NAP in human urine samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CoNP-CDs. TEM images of CoNP-CDs with a scale of (A) 100 nm and (B) 20 nm; (C) The particle size distribution of CoNP-CDs. (D) FTIR spectrum of CoNP-CDs; (E) Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of CoNP-CDs; (F) Influence of excitation wavelength on emission wavelength (The photovoltage was 480 V and the slit width was 5 nm).
XPS spectra of CoNP-CDs and VB12. (A) Full spectrum of total elements. High-resolution XPS spectra of (B) Co 2p, (C) C 1s, (D) N 1s, (E) O 1s, (F) P 2p.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of solution in the presence of CoNP-CDs, CoNP-CDs + TMB, TMB + H2O2, CoNP-CDs + TMB + H2O2. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of solution in the presence of CoNP-CDs + H2O2 with different chromogenic substrate. The EPR spectra of (C)
OH and (D)
O2 captured by DMPO in CoNP-CDs + H2O2 catalytic reaction system.

Optimization of experimental parameters. The relative activity of nanozyme CoNP-CDs changed with (A) hydrothermal temperature, (B) hydrothermal duration during material synthesis, (C) pH, (D) different concentration, (E) reaction temperature and (F) reaction time for measurement by UV–vis spectrophotometer and smartphone.
The steady-state kinetic fitting curves. (A) Catalytic velocity (V) versus TMB concentration (0.08–3.08 mM) curve; (B) Catalytic velocity (V) versus H2O2 concentration (0.15–4.31 mM) curve.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of CoNP-CDs + TMB + H2O2 system with the change of 1-NAP concentration; (B) Photographs and the linear relationships between A652 and 1-NAP concentration; (C) Photographs and the linear relationships between G/(R + B) and 1-NAP concentration; RGB values were exported by software “colorgrab”; (D) Stability; (E) Anti-interference and (F) selectivity of CoNP-CDs.
Schematic illustration of CoNP-CDs for the colorimetric assays of 1-NAP.

head.ce:title: A colorimetric sensor based on multiple elements doped carbon dot nanozyme for rapid detection of 1-naphthol in human urine samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering and Environment, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing 102249, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical Engineering and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical Engineering and Environment, China University of Petroleum, Beijing, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The residual carbaryl in crops can cause serious damage to the human kidney and nervous system after entering the human body, which may be metabolized to 1-naphthol (1-NAP) and excreted through urine. 1-NAP is often used as the biomarker for carbaryl exposure, so the intake or leakage of carbaryl can be monitored by detecting the concentration of 1-NAP. Herein, Co, N, P ternary co-doped carbon dots (CoNP-CDs) derived from vitamin B12 were synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method. CoNP-CDs exhibited oxidase-like activity and excellent peroxidase-like activity, which was attributed to the Fenton-like reaction of Co2+/Co3+ and the presence of pyrrole N and P elements, which together provided multiple active sites for chromogenic substrates. Due to the dual enzyme-like activity of CoNP-CDs, hydroxyl radicals (
OH) and superoxide radicals (
O2 −) were generated during the catalytic process, which could rapidly oxidize colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethyl benzidine (TMB) to blue oxidation products (oxTMB). The α-carbon in 1-NAP can be attacked by
OH, and the catalytic oxidation process of TMB can be inhibited by the consumption of
OH, so that the blue color of the solution became lighter. Based on this principle, a smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensing platform was constructed for the detection of 1-NAP, and which resulted in a linear range of 1.07–37.3 μM and a visual detection limit of 0.68 μM. Moreover, the colorimetric sensing system showed satisfactory recoveries in the detection of human urine samples. The colorimetric sensing system owned the advantages of fast response, strong selectivity and simple operation, and provided a potential strategy for the on-site detection of 1-NAP.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In order to control pests and diseases and regulate crop growth, the use of pesticides in agriculture has gradually increased for boosting crop yield [1,2]. Excessive use of pesticides can lead to pesticide residues in crops and environment [3]. As a carbamate pesticide, carbaryl is widely used because of its obvious control effect on cotton bollworms, rice leafhoppers and other pests [4]. However, the residual carbaryl enters the human body with vegetables, fruits and other crops, which will cause serious damages to the human kidney and nervous system, and even cause cancer [5,6]. People exposed to carbaryl can rapidly metabolize carbaryl to its biomarker 1-NAP within 24 h and excrete it through urine [7]. Monitoring the biomarker is considered to be a recognized method for assessing the risk of pesticide exposure, so the detection of 1-NAP is often used to monitor the intake of carbaryl [8–10].

At present, various methods have been established for the detection of 1-NAP, and mainly include high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [11], gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [12], fluorescence method [13], electrochemical analysis [14,15] and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISAs) [16]. However, most of them usually require expensive instruments, complex pretreatment processes, and long analytical times. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop simple, rapid, economical and efficient detection method for the detection of 1-NAP. In recent years, colorimetric method has been widely concerned because of its obvious phenomenon and simple operation [17]. Natural enzymes can oxidize colorless chromogenic substrates such as TMB, 2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) into blue, green and yellow visible to the naked eye, respectively, which makes the enzymatic method applicable to colorimetry [18,19]. However, the nature of natural enzymes is unstable and expensive, which limits their practical application [20]. Until 2007, Yan’s group first discovered that Fe3O4 nanoparticles could simulate natural enzyme activity [21]. Since then, hundreds of nanomaterials such as transition metal oxides, precious metals, carbon nanomaterials and metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) have been confirmed to have enzyme-like activity [22,23]. These nanomaterials with enzyme-like activity are named as nanozymes. In recent years, the detection of pollutants by nanozymes has absorbed extensive attention.

The exploration of new nanozymes has been aroused in recent years. Carbon nanomaterials have attracted wide attention due to their low biological toxicity, low cost and various sources [24]. As a new type of carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have unique advantages such as small size, easy synthesis, and excellent luminescence properties. They have achieved wide applications in chemistry, food and medical fields due to their rich functional groups and specific recognition sites on the surface for the targets or other mechanisms [25]. Recently, some reports found that a few CDs possessed the ability to simulate the catalytic activity of natural enzymes. By doping elements (metal/nonmetal) in CDs, the electronic and chemical states on the surface of CDs can be changed, the active center sites can be increased, and then the charge transfer between the interfaces can be induced, thereby enhancing their enzyme-like activity [26]. Wang et al. prepared nitrogen and manganese co-doped carbon dots by one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid, ethylenediamine and MnCl2·4H2O as precursors. The CDs had oxidase-like activity, which can oxidize TMB to oxTMB. oxTMB could be diazotized in the presence of nitrite, and the macroscopic performance was that the color of the solution changed from blue to green and finally to yellow. Based on the above principle, a colorimetric detection strategy for nitrite based on CDs was constructed [27]. However, when carbon dots were used as nanozymes, most of them only had a single type of active sites, which led to a slow catalytic rate of the chromogenic substrate. Therefore, it is innovative to design CDs with multiple active sites to enhance their enzyme-like activity.

In this work, CoNP-CDs with dual enzyme-like activity and specific recognition ability to 1-NAP were prepared by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method, and the effects of hydrothermal process on element valence and bonding were studied by characterization. The presence of Co2+/Co3+, pyrrole N and P elements in CoNP-CDs provided multiple active sites, which greatly improved the catalytic activity of nanozymes. As shown in Scheme 1 , CoNP-CDs can produce
OH and
O2 − radicals during the catalytic process, thereby oxidizing TMB to blue oxTMB. The presence of 1-NAP partially consumed
OH, which indirectly inhibited the oxidation process of TMB. The color change of the solution can be observed by the naked eye and then quantified to RGB values by smartphone software. Based on the above principles, a smart phone-assisted sensitive and efficient portable colorimetric detection strategy for 1-NAP was established, which provided a new idea for on-site detection. In addition, satisfactory results were obtained in the actual sample detection of human urine.

The CoNP-CDs were fabricated through one-step hydrothermal method. Briefly, 135.5 mg VB12 was dissolved in 25 mL deionized water. The mixed solution was magnetically stirred for 5 min and then transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (50 mL), which was heated at 180 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the reaction solution was filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane and dialyzed for 48 h with a dialysis bag (MW = 1000), and then the dialysis solution was freeze-dried. Finally, the obtained red solid was dispersed into different concentrations of solution with deionized water and stored at room temperature. Carbon dots synthesized by VB12 at different hydrothermal temperature (160–200 °C) and different hydrothermal time (4–12 h) were utilized for comparison. The information of reagents and instruments for characterization were provided in the supporting information.

The peroxidase-like activity of CoNP-CDs was investigated with TMB as chromogenic substrate. Typically, 160 μL CoNP-CDs (0.09 mg/mL), 920 μL NaAc-HAc buffer solution (0.2 M, pH = 3.5), 100 μL TMB (0.31 mM) and 120 μL H2O2 (3.69 mM) were added to a 2 mL centrifuge tube in turn. The above solution was mixed and reacted at 25 °C for 4 min, and the absorbance at 652 nm was recorded by an UV–vis spectrophotometer. At the same time, the smartphone was used to take photos to record the color change of the solution (The shooting the photos was performed in a Black Box as exhibited in Fig. S1, in which the light source was fixed in inner top, the horizontal distance between the mobile phone and the solution was 40 cm, and the light source was 30 cm away from the solution, the left, right and back were all pure white to exclude the interference of nature lights). The RGB numerical analysis of the above photos was carried out with the mobile phone software ‘colorgrab’.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of 1-NAP by spectroscopy and smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: Colorimetric detection of 1-NAP was performed as follows. Firstly, 120 μL CoNP-CDs (0.09 mg/mL), 880 μL NaAc-HAc buffer solution (0.2 M, pH = 3.5), 100 μL TMB (0.31 mM), 100 μL different concentrations of 1-NAP (0–50 μM) and 100 μL H2O2 (3.69 mM) were added into 2 mL centrifuge tube. The mixed solution was placed at 25 °C for 4 min, and the absorption spectra were recorded by the ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer. Meanwhile, the color change of the solution was recorded as photos with a smartphone as mentioned-above. The RGB numerical analysis of the photos was carried out with the mobile phone software ‘colorgrab’.

The morphology and structure of the prepared materials were examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). It can be seen from Fig. 1 A and B that CoNP-CDs were monodisperse quasi-spheres with small particle size. The average particle size of CoNP-CDs was calculated to be 2.33 ± 0.02 nm, which was consistent with the definition of carbon dots (Fig. 1C). The results showed that carbon dots with uniform particle size were successfully prepared. Subsequently, in order to explore the chemical bonds and surface functional groups, CoNP-CDs were characterized by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FTIR). From Fig. 1D, it can be seen that the absorptions at 3423 cm−1, 3210 cm−1, 1663 cm−1, 1387 cm−1, 1200 cm−1 and 1114 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH, NH, CO/CN, CC, CN and CO, respectively [28]. These results showed that the surface of CoNP-CDs was rich in hydrophilic functional groups, and it may be stable in aqueous solution.

The fluorescence properties of CoNP-CDs were investigated, and the fluorescence 3D contour plot of CoNP-CDs was shown in Fig. S2A. It can be seen that the optimal excitation wavelength of CoNP-CDs was 280 nm, and the corresponding optimal emission wavelength was 375 nm. Subsequently, fluorescence intensities of CoNP-CDs at different pH were measured (pH 3–6 was made because this experiment was suitable for acidic solutions). The results showed that when the solution was adjusted to pH 3, the fluorescence intensity of CoNP-CDs was slightly lower, and the fluorescence intensity was almost unchanged under other pH, indicating that CoNP-CDs were stable in acidic systems (Fig. 1E). When the excitation wavelength increased from 265 nm to 290 nm, the fluorescence intensity of CoNP-CDs firstly increased and then decreased, which had the highest fluorescence intensity at 280 nm. Therefore, 280 nm was optioned as the optimal excitation wavelength of CoNP-CDs, and the corresponding emission wavelength was 375 nm. When the excitation wavelength was changed, the emission wavelength did not change significantly, which indicated that the prepared CoNP-CDs had no excitation dependence (Fig. 1F) [29]. Fig. S2A exhibits the fluorescence 3D contour plot of CoNP-CDs, which also confirmed that there was only one strongest emission with excitation wavelength at 280 nm. The above characterization results showed that the prepared CoNP-CDs had excellent dispersibility and fluorescence stability in aqueous solution.

In order to further explore the change of the valence state of Co element and the bonding form of each element in VB12 during the hydrothermal process, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterization was carried out. Full spectrum of total elements and high-resolution XPS responses of Co 2p, C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, P 2p for VB12 and CoNP-CDs were shown in Fig. 2 A–F. The full spectra of total elements of VB12 and CoNP-CDs were as the same (Fig. 2A). The Co 2p spectra of VB12 and CoNP-CDs were shown in Fig. 2B. In VB12, there were only Co (2p 3/2) and Co (2p 1/2) and their satellite peaks, which were located at 780.2 eV, 784.2 eV, 795.7 eV and 802.0 eV, respectively. However, the Co element in CoNP-CDs existed in two valence states of Co2+ and Co3+, which provided a strong support for the peroxidase activity of the nanozymes [30]. From Fig. 2C, it can be seen that the peaks at 284.4 eV, 285.8 eV and 287.3 eV were attributed to CC/CC, CN/CO and CO [31], respectively. The peak at 398.8 eV in the N 1s high-resolution spectrum of VB12 belonged to pyrrole N, and the peak at 399.9 eV belonged to amide N. In the N 1s high-resolution spectrum of CoNP-CDs, the pyrrole nitrogen shifted to the higher binding energy direction by 0.4 eV, and graphite N appeared at 401.2 eV, which proved that VB12 was graphitized through the high-temperature hydrothermal process (Fig. 2D) [32]. The peaks at 530.8 eV, 532.2 eV and 533.6 eV in the O 1s spectrum of VB12 were attributed to CO, CO bond and OH, respectively. However, the OH peak in CoNP-CDs disappeared and replaced it to generate a new peak at 531.6 eV, which was attributed to PO bond, which may be caused by the dehydration and carbonization of VB12 during hydrothermal process (Fig. 2E) [33]. As shown in Fig. 2F, the peaks of P 2p XPS spectra of VB12 at 132.1 eV and 133.0 eV were attributed to the PO bond and PO bond of the phosphate group. However, CoNP-CDs produced two new peaks at 133.5 eV and 134.5 eV, which were attributed to PN/PC bond and COP bond, respectively, indicating that the P element was successfully doped in CoNP-CDs in the form of bonds [34]. The doping of N and P elements can optimize the distribution of electrons in the carbon structure, and provide sites for the adsorption of the substrate as an electron donor to improve the binding affinity of the reaction substrate, thereby enhancing the catalytic activity of CoNP-CDs [26]. The above results showed that Co, N and P elements have been successfully doped in CoNP-CDs. In addition, compared with VB12, the XPS peaks of the elements of CoNP-CDs after hydrothermal treatment generally shifted to the direction of higher binding energy, which indicated that the hydrothermal process led to the transfer of electrons from O to N and P, which may be due to the polar induction effect of hydroxyl groups [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxidase-like activity and peroxidase-like activity of CoNP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: TMB was used as the chromogenic substrate, and the type of enzyme-like activity of CoNP-CDs was determined by whether H2O2 was added into the reaction system. As shown in Fig. 3 A, when there were only CoNP-CDs in the solution, the absorption spectrum had no obvious absorption peak between 600 and 750 nm, which eliminated the interference of the nanozymes on the color reaction system. When there were only TMB and H2O2 in the solution, the absorption spectrum also had no absorption peak in this range. When CoNP-CDs and TMB coexisted in the solution, the solution became light blue, and the corresponding spectrum had a weak absorption peak at 652 nm, which was the characteristic absorption peak of oxTMB. TMB could be oxidized by CoNP-CDs without H2O2, indicating that CoNP-CDs had oxidase-like activity. When CoNP-CDs, TMB and H2O2 coexisted in the above solution, the solution became dark blue and had an obviously strong absorption peak at 652 nm, indicating that CoNP-CDs had excellent peroxidase-like activity. On the whole, the as-prepared CoNP-CDs owned both oxidase-like activity and peroxidase-like activity. However, its peroxidase-like activity was much stronger than oxidase-like activity, so the peroxidase-like activity of CoNP-CDs was mainly investigated for subsequent experiments. When TMB, OPD and ABTS were used as the oxidation substrates, the solution appeared blue, yellow and green, respectively. The corresponding absorption spectra exhibited characteristic absorption peaks at 652 nm, 450 nm and 425 nm, respectively, indicating that all three substrates could be oxidized (Fig. 3B). The absorption spectrum corresponding to TMB exhibited the largest absorbance, indicating that the system resulted in the best response to TMB. Therefore, TMB was selected as the chromogenic substrate in following experiments.

In order to explore the catalytic mechanism of the enzyme-like activity of CoNP-CDs, the generation of free radical species during the catalytic process was verified by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra. The
OH and
O2 − were captured by adding 5,5,-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO). It could be seen from Fig. 3C that the CoNP-CDs + H2O2 solution produced four typical peaks in the EPR spectrum, and the intensity ratio was about 1:2:2:1, indicating that
OH was produced during the catalytic process. As shown in Fig. 3D, there were four peaks with larger intensity and two peaks with smaller intensity, indicating that
O2 − was also generated during the catalytic process [36]. Combined with the characterization results of XPS and EPR, the possible mechanism of CoNP-CDs with peroxidase-like activity was Fenton-like reaction. Firstly, H2O2 was adsorbed on the surface of CoNP-CDs, and CoNP-CDs catalyzed the OO bond cleavage of H2O2 to produce
OH. Then
OH interacted with H2O2 to produce
O2 −. The
OH and
O2 − produced by the above process eventually oxidize TMB to the blue product (oxTMB) [37].

The absorbance of the solution was recorded by an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer, and the intensity of the absorption peak at 652 nm (A652 nm) was used to represent catalytic activity of the nanozyme. The G/(R + B) value converted from the color of the solution with smartphone software represented the catalytic activity of the nanozyme. The maximum value of each method represented the strongest catalytic activity as 100 %. The ratio of the other absorbance at 652 nm or G/(R + B) to the maximum value was recorded as relative catalytic activity. The results obtained by the two means were collated and compared to optimize the enzyme-like activity of CoNP-CDs. The hydrothermal temperature during the synthesis of the material was firstly optimized. From Fig. 4 A, it was obvious that CoNP-CDs obtained at 180 °C by hydrothermal method exhibited the highest catalytic activity whether it was recorded by UV–vis spectrophotometer or photographing by smartphone. This may be due to the fact that the low temperature did not reach the boiling point of VB12, and the excessively high temperature may lead to excessive carbonization and collapse of the pyrrole structure [38]. The carbonization and nucleation of CDs needed a certain time, and the hydrothermal time may also affect the structure and particle size of CDs. Therefore, the hydrothermal time was optimized, and the results exhibited that the hydrothermal time was controlled at 12 h, and the obtained CDs exhibiting the best relative activity, and more time would result in no remarkable change of the relative activity. The optimum hydrothermal time was selected as 12 h (Fig. 4B). Subsequently, the experimental parameters of the catalytic reaction were optimized, including the pH value of the buffer solution (pH 3.0–7.0), the concentration of nanozyme (0.01–0.15 mg/mL), the reaction temperature (10–35 °C), and the reaction time. The pH value of the solution can directly affect the electron loss ability of the chromogenic substrate, thus affecting the oxidation process. From Fig. 4C, it can be seen that the nanozyme had the highest catalytic ability when the buffer solution pH was adjusted to pH 3.5. Subsequently, the concentration of nanozymes was optimized. When the concentration of CoNP-CDs was lower than 0.09 mg/mL, the catalytic activity of CoNP-CDs increased with the increase of concentration of CoNP-CDs. When the concentration of CoNP-CDs was between 0.09 and 0.14 mg/mL, the enzyme-like activity was almost unchanged. However, when the concentration of CoNP-CDs was too high, the enzyme-like activity showed a downward trend, which may be due to the self-aggregation of CoNP-CDs leading to the reduction of active sites (Fig. 4D) [39]. In addition, when the concentration of CoNP-CDs was higher than 0.12 mg/mL, the decrease of relative catalytic activity was more obvious when the signal obtained with the smartphone was used to represent its relative activity. This was because CoNP-CDs themselves were light pink, which would have a certain impact on the RGB value of the color of the solution. Temperature is also an important factor affecting the reaction rate. As shown in Fig. 4E, the nanozyme CoNP-CDs had the highest catalytic activity at 25 °C. With the optimum parameters, the system almost reached the reaction equilibrium at 4 min (Fig. 4F). Therefore, 4 min was optioned as the reaction end point for the determination of UV absorption spectrum and photographing. It can be seen clearly from the above optimization results that when G/(R + B) and A652 nm represented the relative activity of the nanozyme, the two means showed similar regularity (difference within 6.60 %). The above results showed that G/(R + B) algorithm can accurately represent A652 nm to a certain extent, which provided a strong support for the development of smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensors.

It is known that Km and Vmax are two key parameters for quantifying enzyme catalytic ability. KM and Vmax are usually calculated by Eq. (1):
(1)
V =
V
m a x
[ S ]
/
(
K m
+
[ S ]
)
where V (M/s) is the initial reaction rate, Vmax (M/s) is the maximum reaction rate, [S] (mM) is the substrate concentration, and Km (mM) is the Michaelis-Menten constant [40]. Among them, Km and Vmax are important reference standards for judging the superiority of enzymes. Km is the substrate concentration when the enzymatic reaction reaches half of the maximum reaction rate, which represents the affinity of the enzyme to the substrate. The smaller Km value indicates the higher affinity of the enzyme to the substrate. Vmax is the reaction rate when the enzyme and the substrate reach saturation, representing the catalytic activity of the enzyme. The larger the Vmax value, the stronger the catalytic activity of the enzyme. As shown in Fig. 5 A and B, when TMB and H2O2 were used as substrates for enzymatic reaction, the Michaelis-Menten constants (Km) were 1.76 mM and 0.26 mM, respectively, indicating that H2O2 had higher affinity with CoNP-CDs than TMB. The maximum reaction rates (Vmax) were 55.97 × 10−8 M/s and 35.01 × 10−8 M/s, respectively. The above results showed that the concentration of TMB had a greater influence on the reaction rate of enzymatic reaction than H2O2. Meanwhile, compared with other reported nanozymes with peroxidase-like activity (Table S1), the CoNP-CDs prepared in this work have stronger affinity with the substrate and faster reaction rate. The reaction temperature of other carbon dot nanozymes should be controlled at 37–45 °C. In contrast, CoNP-CDs had higher enzyme activity in the range of 25–35 °C, which was more in line with the room temperature conditions for actual detection. In addition, when the enzyme activity reached the best, the response time of other carbon dot nanozymes was about 20–60 min, while the response time of this present sensor was only 4 min, so the as-prepared CoNP-CDs nanozyme had satisfactory catalytic performance and resulted in rapid detection and saving analytical time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of 1-NAP by UV–visible spectroscopy and smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal conditions, the detection performance of the colorimetric sensor based on the prepared CoNP-CDs nanozyme for 1-NAP was performed. The UV–visible absorption spectra of CoNP-CDs-TMB-H2O2 reaction system in the presence of different concentrations of 1-NAP were shown in Fig. 6 A. The results showed that the absorbance of the solution gradually decreased with the increase of 1-NAP concentration. In the range of 0.53–53.4 μM, the absorbance of the solution at 652 nm showed a good linear relationship with the concentration of 1-NAP (Fig. 6B). The linear equation was A652 nm = −0.0491C1-NAP + 3.6883, and the spectral detection limit was 0.23 μM (3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation of fifteen blank samples, k is the slope of the established standard curve).

The smartphone software ‘colorgrab’ was used to transform the color of the solution as RGB values, and the G/(R + B) value changed with the concentration of 1-NAP (Fig. 6C). When the concentration of 1-NAP was over the range of 1.07–37.3 μM, a good linear relationship was achieved. The linear equation was G/(R + B) = −0.0142C1-NAP + 1.0773, and the colorimetric detection limit was 0.68 μM. The above results showed that the colorimetric sensing system based on CoNP-CDs nanozymes had good performance in detecting 1-NAP. These results were also compared with some reported colorimetric detection results of 1-NAP and carbaryl (Table S2). Satisfactory results were obtained in terms of linear range, LOD and response time. In summary, the 1-NAP colorimetric determination strategy based on CoNP-CDs possessed the advantages of good sensitivity, high efficiency and rapid response.

The catalytic stability of nanozymes is an important factor affecting their practical application. Therefore, the relative enzyme-like activity of CoNP-CDs was investigated every 10 days. The results showed that the relative activity of CoNP-CDs was almost unchanged within 60 days, indicating that the nanozymes prepared in this work had good stability (Fig. 6D). In addition, the stability of 1-NAP and TMB in pH 3.5 buffer solution was verified. It can be seen from Fig. S3A that the buffer solution itself had no fluorescence, so the fluorescence signals in Fig. S3B and C belonged to 1-NAP and TMB, respectively. Therefore, the stability of the two compounds was judged by the change of fluorescence emission intensity at the optimal excitation wavelength. It can be seen from Fig. S4A and B that the emission peak intensity of 1-NAP did not change within 30 min, while the emission peak intensity of TMB decreased slightly, which was consistent with most of the literature. This is because NH2 in TMB was more likely to lose electrons at low pH [41]. Then, the relative fluorescence intensity was plotted to show the change of fluorescence intensity more intuitively. As shown in Fig. S4C, the relative fluorescence intensity of 1-NAP remained unchanged within 30 min, indicating that 1-NAP was stable in buffer solution. Although the fluorescence intensity of TMB decreased slightly, the relative fluorescence intensity remained above 92.0 % at 30 min, and the relative fluorescence intensity remained above 98.5 % within 5 min (Fig. S4D). Since the colorimetric detection only took 4 min, the reaction results were almost not generated. Then, common ions such as Na+, K+, Ca2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Cl−, I−, and NO3 − were selected to test the anti-interference of the reaction system, and the RGB values were converted for calculation after being photographed with the smartphone. It can be clearly seen from Fig. 6E that the color of the solution in the presence of the above ions was consistent with that of the blank sample, and the G/(R + B) value was almost the same as that of the blank group, indicating that the system had excellent anti-interference. The selectivity of nanozymes to the target is crucial, which can greatly reduce the false positives in actual detection and make the results more reliable. Therefore, eight phenolic compounds with similar structure to 1-NAP were selected for specific detection, respectively. It can be seen that only the presence of 1-NAP inhibited the oxidation process of TMB, resulting in a lighter blue solution (Fig. 6F). This may be due to the fact that compared with other phenols, the phenyl structure in 1-NAP produced π-π conjugation with the pyrrole and benzene ring structures in CoNP-CDs, which reduced the active sites of nanozymes and inhibited the catalytic activity [42]. The difference between 2-NAP and 1-NAP is that the OH of the former is located on the β-carbon, while the OH of the latter is located on the α-carbon. The charge density of carbon atoms on the naphthalene ring directly affects the electrophilic attack of
OH. The total charge density (ΣC) of all carbon atoms of 1-NAP is −0.161, which is more negative than that of 2-NAP (−0.153). The above results indicated that the activation of α-OH was stronger than that of β-OH, so 1-NAP was more prone to oxidation under the attack of oxidant [43]. In summary, the colorimetric sensing strategy developed in this paper had excellent selectivity for 1-NAP.

The principle of the detection system for 1-NAP was explored. After the addition of 1-NAP to the detection system, the oxidation process of TMB was inhibited mainly due to the following three reasons. Firstly, 1-NAP can be adsorbed onto the surface of CoNP-CDs by hydrogen bonds between the hydroxyl groups of 1-NAP and the hydroxyl, amino and carboxyl groups on CoNP-CDs, and occupy the active sites, which decrease the enzyme-like activity of CoNP-CDs, and inhibit the generation of the active species. Secondly, 1-NAP can form hydrogen bonds with TMB, which also leads to decrease the number of TMB molecules migrating onto the surface of CoNP-CDs occurring the oxidation reaction. Thirdly, 1-NAP molecules were also oxidated by the active oxygen species generated in the nanozyme system, and which declined the number of active oxygen species for oxidating TMB. In order to further explore the interaction between CoNP-CDs and 1-NAP, relevant experiments were carried out. The fluorescence 3D contour plot of CoNP-CDs and 1-NAP were shown in Figs. S2A and S3B. The optimal excitation wavelength of CoNP-CDs was 280 nm, and the emission wavelengths were in the range of 300–425 nm. At the same conditions, the emission of 1-NAP was very poor. After adding 1-NAP, the fluorescence intensity of CoNP-CDs did not increase, but decreased slightly (Fig. S2B). Since the characteristic absorption wavelength of 1-NAP was 293 nm (Fig. S5), which coincided with the excitation wavelength of CoNP-CDs, the fluorescence quenching of CoNP-CDs may be caused by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filtering effect (IFE), which may be due to the consumption of active groups of NH2, OH and COOH on the surface of CoNP-CDs by forming hydrogen bonds with OH groups of 1-NAP [44]. On the other hand,
OH and
O2 − generated by the catalytic reaction preferentially attacked the α-carbon of 1-NAP. Then 1-NAP was converted to aldehydes and acids or further ring-opening under the combined action of
OH and
O2 − [45]. This process consumed a large amount of free radicals, resulting in the oxidation process being inhibited again, and which made the blue color change of the solution become visible to the naked eye. Under the combination of the above factors, the colorimetric sensor had excellent sensitivity to the detection of 1-NAP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of 1-NAP in human urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the feasibility of the proposed colorimetric sensing system based on CoNP-CDs nanozymes in practical detection, the urine samples of three volunteers were taken as actual samples. The maximum safe daily intake of carbaryl was 8 μg/kg. The concentration of 1-NAP in urine samples of volunteers after taking the maximum safe dose of carbaryl was determined. The results showed that the concentration of 1-NAP in urine samples ranged from 16 to 58 μmoL/moL creatinine [46]. The average content of creatinine per liter of urine was about 12 mmoL, so the safe concentration of 1-NAP in urine was calculated to be less than 0.70 μM. Therefore, when the concentration of 1-NAP in urine exceeded 0.70 μM, it can be judged that it exceeded the pesticide residue limit. The visual detection limit of 1-NAP in this work was 0.68 μM, so 1-NAP could be detected when its concentration exceeded the maximum safety threshold. In addition, before the actual detection of human urine, anti-interference experiments were carried out on several common substances (UA, OA, ALB, CREA and Urea) in urine to ensure the accuracy of colorimetric results. As shown in Fig. S6, the above five substances at the concentration of 1 mM (the concentration was much higher than the concentration in the diluted urine) had almost no interference with the chromogenic system. The urine samples were diluted 50 times with NaAc-HAc buffer solution as the pretreatment process, and then the recoveries and relative standard deviations (RSDs) were calculated by standard spiked method. As shown in Table S3, the recoveries of UV-absorption spectroscopy and smartphone-assisted colorimetric methods were in the range of 98.2–103.5 % and 94.8–107.1 %, the RSDs were less than 2.41 % and 2.83 %, respectively. The above results showed that the developed colorimetric sensing system had acceptable sensitivity and repeatability for the detection of 1-NAP in actual urine samples, which provided a certain application potential for clinical diagnosis.

In summary, Co, N, P ternary co-doped-CDs were designed and constructed by a simple one-pot hydrothermal method using VB12 as a precursor. CoNP-CDs exhibited both oxidase-like activity and excellent peroxidase-like activity, which was attributed to the multiple active sites provided by the Co2+/Co3+ Fenton-like reaction, pyrrole N and P elements. CoNP-CDs can generate
OH and
O2 − in the catalytic process, and oxidize TMB within 4 min. The oxidation of TMB was inhibited by the consumption of reactive oxygen species by 1-NAP, and the color change of the solution was visible to the naked eye. Based on the above principles, a smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensing strategy for ultra-fast and sensitive detection of 1-NAP was established, which achieved good visual detection limit of 0.68 μM for 1-NAP and showed acceptable results in the detection of human urine. The experimental results demonstrated that the conceived sensor provided a new idea for the rapid and specific detection of 1-NAP.

Linchun Nie: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Shuangying Li: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Data curation. Liushan Jiang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Lutong Bu: Software, Investigation. Guangyu Dong: Validation, Software, Investigation. Denghao Song: Investigation, Formal analysis. Jiawei Liao: Validation, Software, Investigation. Guojin Tang: Software, Methodology, Investigation. Qingxiang Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Third Comprehensive Scientific Investigation Project of Xinjiang (2022xjkk1002) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21976211).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2024.08.244.

ce:section.ce:para:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Urinary naphthol as a biomarker of exposure: results from an oral exposure to carbaryl and workers occupationally exposed to naphthalene
Toxics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Sams, Urinary naphthol as a biomarker of exposure: results from an oral exposure to carbaryl and workers occupationally exposed to naphthalene, Toxics 5 (2017) 3.
